lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced custom software design, development, modification, testing, integration, maintenance, and support to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal development teams, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, packaged-software publishers, pure product or SaaS companies, and businesses primarily classified as systems integration, facilities management, hosting, or temporary staffing rather than custom programming.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and government customers purchase custom projects, agile development capacity, dedicated teams, enhancements, maintenance, and support under time-and-materials, labor-hour, fixed-price, milestone, retainer, or managed-capacity arrangements.

a.bridge: The exact-industry occupation mix identifies where wages sit but does not measure task exposure, while controlled AI studies and developer surveys measure selected coding tasks or global developers rather than the wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task share in LMM 541511 firms. The range applies the greatest exposure to coding, testing, debugging, documentation, research, and routine support; moderate exposure to analysis, project administration, sales, and management; and lower exposure to client discovery, architecture, security judgment, deployment accountability, and relationship work. Coding experiments can bias exposure upward when tasks are greenfield and bounded, while mature-repository results and high current adoption can bias the remaining opportunity downward because some easy gains are already captured.
a.rationale: Computer and mathematical occupations represent 56% of exact-industry employment, including 27% software developers and programmers, 10% computer and information analysts, 8% support specialists, and 7% database and systems administrators or network architects; management, business and financial, office support, and sales add another 36%. O*NET shows that developer work combines code and testing with requirements analysis, design, coordination, support, and consequential judgment. A narrow controlled task was completed 55.8% faster with Copilot, but experienced maintainers on their own repositories were 19% slower with early-2025 tools. The base therefore recognizes broad task exposure without equating code generation with whole-job substitution.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-wage study of 541511 firms that maps current workflow steps by occupation, current AI automation, billability, complexity, and verified labor hours avoided would materially change this range.
caveats: The occupation shares are employment-weighted; wage weighting is inferred because public exact-industry wage detail is incomplete on the fetched page.
Developer surveys are global and self-selected, and controlled experiments cover narrower tasks than client software delivery.
The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization factor, so the combined labor opportunity is sensitive to that stated vintage mismatch.

rho.bridge: DORA and Stack Overflow measure adoption, reliance, sentiment, and self-reported productivity among global technology professionals, not the five-year implemented share of the exposed opportunity in US LMM custom-programming firms. The estimate treats widespread current use as evidence of technical readiness, then discounts for code review, security, customer confidentiality, tool integration, legacy context, testing, governance, and organizational change. Digitally sophisticated survey respondents may bias readiness upward relative to smaller firms, while rapid tool improvement over five years may make present implementation constraints too pessimistic.
rho.rationale: DORA's 2025 survey of nearly 5,000 technology professionals reported 90% AI adoption, 65% moderate-to-heavy reliance, and more than 80% self-reported productivity improvement. Stack Overflow found only 52% reported a positive productivity effect, 46% distrusted AI accuracy, and resistance was greatest for project planning and deployment or monitoring. Since AI is already embedded in core development workflows but dependable production use requires verification and systems change, a majority rather than all of the exposed opportunity is expected to be implemented within five years.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal panel of US 541511 firms reporting production deployment by workflow, accepted AI-generated changes, cycle time, rework, defects, security incidents, utilization, and net labor hours avoided would materially move this estimate.
caveats: Adoption and time spent with AI are not the same as realized labor substitution or avoided hiring.
Self-reported productivity can diverge from measured productivity, as the METR experiment demonstrates.
Customer-specific data restrictions and mature legacy codebases may constrain implementation more than greenfield work.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition itself is customer-specific outsourced programming, and the industry product mix spans custom application, website, database, software customization, technical support, and related development services. Most genuine LMM firms should therefore fit the external-service lens. The range excludes one-off or owner-dependent shops without repeat delivery systems, staffing-heavy misclassifications, captive entities, product businesses classified imperfectly, firms with nontransferable customer relationships, and firms placed in the EBITDA band incorrectly by the dataset's margin bridge.
e.change_evidence: A stratified audit of the injected firm population using private-company databases and firm websites, followed by interviews or quality-of-earnings data on recurring or repeat customers, revenue by service, owner dependence, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and contract assignability, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No public source directly measures eligibility under the full outsourced-repeat-service and LMM lens.
The injected firm count is an estimate derived from revenue size classes and a broad services margin, so the denominator may include firms outside the actual EBITDA band.
Project work can be repeatable at the firm level even when individual customer contracts are not recurring, making classification judgment material.

s5.bridge: Capstone's AppDev series covers global software and application-development services targets of all sizes, while Hyde Park Capital covers broader US IT services; neither isolates qualifying control transfers of eligible LMM 541511 firms. The estimate uses their sustained transaction activity and services-target share as evidence of liquidity, then removes software-product targets, managed services, systems integrators, non-US deals, growth financings, large sellers, non-control transactions, and ineligible firms. Broader category inclusion biases the raw incidence upward, while private small-company deals missing from announced databases bias it downward.
s5.rationale: Capstone counted 238 AppDev transactions year-to-date in 2025, with services representing 59.2% of targets and private strategic or sponsor-backed buyers prominent. Hyde Park Capital reported 734 US IT-services deals in 2024, still above pre-pandemic 2019 volume, and identified software development capabilities as an active acquisition theme. Relative to the injected LMM population, these broad deal counts support a meaningful but minority five-year probability of a qualifying transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of announced and private US 541511 transactions matched to unique sellers, with seller EBITDA, service-versus-product revenue, transaction structure, control status, and repeat ownership transfers, would materially change the range.
caveats: The transaction taxonomies are materially broader than six-digit NAICS 541511 and include firms outside the target EBITDA range.
Published deal databases undercount confidential small transactions but include financings or targets that do not meet the control-transfer definition.
The injected firm denominator is itself an EBITDA-band estimate rather than an observed eligible-firm census.

q.rationale: Custom software is sold under both fixed-price and time-and-materials or labor-hour structures. Government guidance notes that fixed price creates a strong contractor incentive to control cost when requirements are identifiable, whereas uncertain agile work can use time-and-materials or labor-hour contracts. Automation savings are retained most directly during a fixed-price term, but fewer hours reduce revenue under labor-based billing; renewals, competitive bids, rate-card transparency, scope renegotiation, and customer bargaining share gains over five years. A mixed contract portfolio supports retention below one-half in the base case.
q.change_evidence: Revenue-weighted contract data from representative 541511 firms, segmented by fixed-price, milestone, time-and-materials, dedicated-team, retainer, and outcome-based work, paired with post-AI renewal pricing and realized project margins, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The cited contracting guidance concerns US federal agile procurement and does not measure private-market revenue mix.
No reliable public source quantifies exact-industry contract shares or multi-year repricing behavior.
Offshore competition, customer insourcing, and vendor-specific bargaining power can move retention widely.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for the broader computer systems design and related services industry, NAICS 541500, rather than exact six-digit 541511. Converting its 2024-to-2034 output ratio of 1,096.2/799.0 to a five-year ratio yields about 1.17, used as the base. The broader group includes systems design, facilities management, and other computer services; stronger infrastructure and managed-services growth could bias it above custom programming, while new application demand and AI transformation work could make it understate 541511 growth.
d5.rationale: BLS projects 3.2% annual real output growth and 15.8% employment growth over 2024-2034 for the broader computer systems design and related services industry. Custom programming should benefit from digital modernization, cloud migration, data engineering, cybersecurity, and demand for AI-enabled applications, while weak technology budgets, offshoring, customer insourcing, reusable software, and AI self-service could reduce demand for the current service basket. The range therefore includes slight contraction and substantial expansion around the broader-industry projection.
d5.change_evidence: An exact 541511 forecast of real revenue or output by service line, deflated with an industry-appropriate price index and adjusted for changes in software quality and scope, would materially change the range; a panel of bookings, backlog, cancellations, and customer technology budgets would tighten it.
caveats: BLS output is available only for broader NAICS 541500, not exact 541511.
Constant-quality measurement is difficult when software capability, development speed, and delivered scope change rapidly.
Demand may diverge sharply across government, healthcare, financial services, industrial, consumer, and small-business customers.

o.rationale: A substantial majority of year-five custom-programming quantity should still require an accountable operator to elicit requirements, design architecture, integrate systems, protect data, test behavior, deploy safely, maintain production code, and coordinate with customer teams. O*NET emphasizes analysis, decisions, design, collaboration, support, accuracy, and standards compliance alongside programming. Stack Overflow respondents remain especially resistant to AI-led project planning and deployment or monitoring and widely require human verification. Basic websites, prototypes, routine integrations, simple applications, documentation, and some maintenance can nevertheless be eliminated, self-served with AI or low-code tools, or supplied as packaged software.
o.change_evidence: A representative customer cohort measuring custom-development spend displaced by internal AI teams, low-code platforms, packaged software, or autonomous agents, together with loss reasons from 541511 proposals and renewals, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No public study directly measures the five-year share of custom-programming demand that still requires an external operating firm.
AI capability may advance nonlinearly, particularly for bounded greenfield applications, while legacy integration and accountability may remain stubborn.
This construct distinguishes operator displacement from labor efficiency; the same AI tools can raise productivity without eliminating the service provider.

narrative.executive_view: Custom programming is a labor-intensive outsourced service whose core production tasks are already being reshaped by AI. The strongest opportunity is faster delivery and avoided hiring across coding, testing, debugging, documentation, and support, while requirements, architecture, security, deployment, and customer accountability keep a capable operator in the loop.
narrative.ai_impact: AI assistance is already common, but results vary from large gains on bounded coding tasks to slower performance in mature repositories. Over five years, integrated agents should automate more of the development lifecycle, yet verification, context, data controls, and organizational workflow design will determine whether tool use becomes dependable operating leverage.
narrative.value_capture: Commercial capture is the central constraint. Fixed-price and milestone work can preserve delivery savings within a contract, while time-and-materials, labor-hour, and dedicated-team models translate fewer hours into lower revenue; competitive renewals and customer bargaining will share gains even where initial margins rise.
narrative.firm_availability: The NAICS definition aligns closely with outsourced customer work, so many genuine LMM firms should meet the lens. The practical pool narrows for owner-dependent boutiques, staffing-heavy businesses, product-like companies, concentrated accounts, weak documentation, and firms whose actual EBITDA falls outside the range implied by the dataset margin bridge.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS expects strong real growth for the broader computer-services group, supported by modernization, cloud, data, cybersecurity, and AI application work. Demand for the current basket is less certain because reusable platforms, packaged software, offshoring, insourcing, and customer self-service can absorb simpler projects even as complex transformation demand expands.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are exact wage-weighted task exposure, real production productivity rather than perceived gains, contract mix, and the share of announced IT deals that are qualifying 541511 transfers. A poor outcome would combine mature-code productivity limits and security constraints with labor-hour billing, rapid price competition, customer self-service, and a seller pool dominated by owner relationships or undifferentiated staffing.
narrative.principal_driver: A majority-computer occupation mix and rapidly improving coding agents create broad scope for faster delivery and avoided technical hiring.
narrative.principal_weakness: Labor-based billing and intense global competition can pass automation gains to customers before the operator retains them.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 541511 Custom Computer Programming Services
sources.supports: Exact-industry definition and boundaries: writing, modifying, testing, and supporting software for a particular customer, distinct from packaged software and systems design.

sources.title: Custom Computer Programming Services - Market Size, Financial Statistics, Industry Trends
sources.supports: Exact-industry occupation shares and service mix, including computer and mathematical, software development, analysis, support, management, business, sales, and office roles.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Software Developers
sources.supports: Software-developer tasks and work requirements spanning programming, requirements analysis, design, testing, coordination, support, judgment, accuracy, and standards compliance.

sources.title: The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
sources.supports: Controlled experiment finding that developers with Copilot completed a bounded JavaScript HTTP-server task 55.8% faster.

sources.title: 2025 Stack Overflow Developer Survey: AI
sources.supports: Developer AI workflow use, planned adoption, self-reported productivity, trust, agent use, and resistance in high-responsibility development tasks.

sources.title: We Are Changing Our Developer Productivity Experiment Design
sources.supports: METR's measured early-2025 slowdown for experienced open-source developers, later-study uncertainty, selection effects, and evidence that productivity effects are changing.

sources.title: How Are Developers Using AI? Inside Google's 2025 DORA Report
sources.supports: Survey size, AI adoption, reliance, self-reported productivity and code quality, trust, and the importance of organizational systems for implementation.

sources.title: AppDev Market M&A Update
sources.supports: 2025 AppDev transaction volume, target and buyer mix, services-segment share, and acquisition interest in custom application development and AI transformation services.

sources.title: IT Services M&A Report: Spring 2025
sources.supports: US IT-services deal volume through 2024 and acquisition themes including AI, data, cloud, cybersecurity, and software development capabilities.

sources.title: TechFAR Hub: Contract Design
sources.supports: Use and incentives of firm-fixed-price, time-and-materials, and labor-hour structures for agile software development, plus testing and customer-team responsibilities.

sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry
sources.supports: BLS 2024-2034 employment and constant-dollar output projections for broader NAICS 541500 computer systems design and related services.
